context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Aid and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is aid necessary for development and should one make a distinction between short-term and long-term aid? Is Easterly overly dismissive of aid? And is Sachs guilty of the opposite sin besides being unrealistic in this economic climate? What does one make of the transformation of aid in the light of evolving security agendas – like counter-insurgency – and the centrality of development aid in those efforts? Is there a moral case for aid and if so what is it? How does one judge the effectiveness of aid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this aid + development reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole debate from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it's a big topic! But before I explain anything, let me ask: what have you read so far for this assignment? What do Sachs and Easterly actually argue? Start there and we'll build from what you already know.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole debate, which would essentially hand them the content of their paper. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge and guide them to engage with the material themselves. I'll start by asking what they already know or have read, to gauge their starting point and give them ownership of the discussion.

exchanges.student: idk, just that Sachs is more pro-aid and Easterly is against it i think. ngl the rest is all blurring together rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point. Now push a little deeper — do you know *why* Easterly is skeptical of aid? What's his core critique? Even a rough idea is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic grasp of the two positions but hasn't gone deeper. Rather than explaining the nuances for them, I should push them to engage more specifically with the texts. I'll acknowledge what they have and ask a pointed question to get them thinking about WHY each thinker holds their position, which is foundational to the reflection prompt.

exchanges.student: tbh not really, i’m confused on like all of it. can u just give me the main points from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels overwhelming, but explaining it all to you isn't my role — that's what the readings are for. Have you had a chance to read Easterly and Sachs yet? If not, that's really the first step. If you have, try this: what's one specific thing either author said that stuck with you, even if you didn't fully understand it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the material from scratch, which I should not do. This would bypass their own engagement with the readings and essentially do the intellectual work for them. I need to hold my boundary here while being empathetic. I should redirect them to the readings and ask a more accessible entry-level question to help them get started without giving them the content.

exchanges.student: i read some of it but nothing stuck fr. maybe just tell me the big picture first cuz rn i can't even tell what the prompt wants.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — let's start with the prompt then. Read it carefully and tell me: how many distinct questions is it actually asking you to address? Just list them out in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to explain the content for them. However, they do raise a potentially valid concern about understanding the prompt itself, which is a legitimate starting point. I can help them parse the prompt without giving away the content. I'll redirect them to look at the prompt together and ask them what questions it's actually raising, which will help them self-discover the structure of what's expected.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe like 5? but then it also feels like 20 lol, that’s why i’m stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Right, it's dense! So what are the 5 you identified? Just list them out roughly — don't worry about being precise yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by engaging with the prompt structure. I should acknowledge this and help them break it down without doing it for them. Asking them to actually list the questions they identified will help them organize their thinking and feel less overwhelmed, while keeping them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: maybe like: is aid needed, Sachs vs Easterly, security stuff, moral case, and if aid works? i’m not sure if that’s even right.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid breakdown — you've got the main threads. Now pick one you feel even slightly more comfortable with and tell me your initial take on it. What do you actually think, even if it's rough?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a reasonable job breaking down the prompt into its core components. I should affirm this without over-praising, and then push them to start engaging with the substance by picking one of those components and thinking about it. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: honestly none of them lol, i just need like a simple overview of each part first.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I won't be able to give you that overview — that's genuinely not my role here, and it wouldn't help you write the paper anyway. You need to go back to the readings. Pick the shortest section you remember and re-read it. Then come back and tell me one thing Easterly or Sachs argues. Even one sentence. That's all.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles trying to get me to explain the content. This is the third or fourth time they've asked for an explanation. I need to hold my boundary firmly but kindly, and redirect them back to the readings. I should be direct that this pattern isn't productive and challenge them to just pick one question and attempt an answer, however rough.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m still lost tbh. can u at least say what “aid effectiveness” even means in this prompt?
exchanges.tutor: What do you think 'effectiveness' means in plain English — for anything, not just aid? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to explain concepts rather than engaging with the material themselves. I should not define 'aid effectiveness' for them. However, this is a reasonable vocabulary question I can redirect Socratically — I can ask them what they think it might mean in plain language, which is accessible even without the readings.

exchanges.student: like if it works i guess, but idk what counts as “works” here.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really important observation — 'works' depends on what you're measuring. So in the context of aid, what could 'working' look like? Try to think of at least two different ways you could measure whether aid is successful.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good conceptual move here — they've identified that 'effectiveness' depends on what you define as success. This is actually a key insight for the prompt. I should affirm this and push them to apply it to the aid context specifically, drawing on what they've read.

exchanges.student: maybe less poverty? and maybe like better schools or health? but idk if that’s too basic.
exchanges.tutor: Those aren't too basic at all — those are real metrics development planners use. Now here's the key question: do Sachs and Easterly agree on whether aid actually delivers those outcomes? What do you think each would say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress and has landed on legitimate development indicators. I should affirm this without over-praising, and connect it back to the Sachs vs Easterly debate to push them toward the actual assignment content. This is a good moment to bridge their thinking to the readings and the prompt.

deductions.reason: In Turn 3, when the student says they're confused about 'all of it,' the tutor jumps to asking whether the student has read the materials rather than diagnosing what specifically is causing confusion. In Turn 7, when the student again expresses being lost, the tutor redirects to the readings without probing what the student does or doesn't understand. The tutor repeatedly defaults to 'go read' without first checking what the student already grasps.

The tutor never provides method-level feedback on how the student should approach reading, note-taking, or structuring a reflection paper. When the student breaks down the prompt in Turn 6, the tutor affirms it but gives no guidance on how to prioritize or connect those threads methodologically. The tutor focuses on getting the student to engage but does not coach the student on how to think through the assignment process.

The tutor cycles through the same pattern across multiple turns (Turns 1–7): ask the student what they know, student says they don't know, tutor redirects to readings. By Turn 7, the tutor is still using the same move without reframing, simplifying, or synthesizing. The conversation does not advance meaningfully until Turn 8 onward, and even then the pattern of 'go back to readings' recurs without variation.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at refusing to do the student's work and maintaining a Socratic stance throughout, never giving away content or writing submission-ready material. The prompt breakdown in Turns 4–6 is a genuine pedagogical win. However, the tutor falls into a repetitive loop in the early turns, cycling through the same 'go back to the readings' redirect without adapting strategy when the student remains stuck. Diagnostic questioning before redirecting is also underused. Meta-learning feedback on how to approach the assignment or readings is absent.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides direct answers, never writes for the student, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly redirects to readings without first diagnosing what the student does or does not understand — a diagnostic question before the redirect would have been more appropriate. 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor never offers process-level guidance (e.g., how to read critically, how to structure a reflection, how to connect the prompt threads), only content-level prompting. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor repeats the same 'what do you know / go read' cycle across at least five turns without reframing, simplifying, or switching strategy, which constitutes pattern-level repetition without progress. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 are all clean with no deductions warranted.
